titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary Davidson, 1 February 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara Mary. In this short and rushed letter, James sends his fondest love and wishes to his mother and family as his company makes arrangements to move back to action in the trenches. Unlike the conditions and fighting he has already experienced in previous locations, he notes that the trenches his company will now be posted to are located at a very bad part of the front line.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
1/2/16 My dearest Mother â We
start for the trenches to-
morrow so I am sure
you will excuse a
very wee line as you
can understand what
a lot I have to attend
to. It was bad enough
trying to get my Coy
organised & into running
order but now on the
top of it all this comes
the order to move & with
it of course all the extra
preparations. I simply
don't know what way
to turn but everyone
is working hard &
so things are gradually
getting straightened
out. You need
not be anxious if
you don't hear from
me for a week or more
as there will be little time
for writing until we have
actually taken over &
as it were settled down. Please thank K. for her
letter â was so glad
to hear that she is able
to be out of doors a little.
What bad luck about
poor wee Binkie, I fear
there is not much chance
of him recovering from
such a bad accident. The parcels all arrive
safely & regularly & I
have no alterations to
suggest meantime in
the contents.
On Active Service
RCd 7th Feb 16 Mrs S. C. Davidson Seaport Bangor Co Down Ireland
J Davidson
